content.full_text:
LEE E. WELCH
1520 Honor Oak Lane
Yuba City, California 95993

October 2, 1991

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

RE: Request for Advisory Opinion of the Fair Political
    Practices Commission

Gentlemen:

I respectfully request of the Fair Political Practices
Commission under the Political Reform Act of 1974 and as permitted
under the act an advisory opinion on the following questions:

1. Does my occupation as a real estate broker who owns and
manages a local real estate firm in the Yuba City area and at the
same time serving as a member of the Yuba City Planning Commission
in and of itself and without regard to specific projects,
applications, rezonings, permit approvals, subdivision approvals
and the like, namely in a vacuum, constitute a conflict of interest
which would effectively preclude me from serving on the Commission
on a theory that there is somehow some inherent incompatibility or
conflict between serving on a local planning commission and at the
same time owning and being the managing broker of an active local
real estate firm.

2. Assuming that after I have voted on a particular planning
issue in which I was satisfied that I had no economic interest,
either direct or indirect, at the time the decision was made in
that particular planning, zoning or subdivision matter and
parenthetically one in which I neither anticipated, suggested, was
promised or assured of any future financial source of income and
subsequently either one of my real estate agents and/or myself for
that matter had occasion to represent a buyer in the multiple
listings service cooperative sale on the project that was the
subject of the prior approval and/or somehow subsequently became
involved in a subsequent listing of that property or some portion
thereof for sale or for resale, would that somehow taint my prior
participation in that planning decision so as to now constitute a
conflict of interest which would somehow preclude me or prohibit me
from participating in the listing of that property or the sale of
that property under the multiple listing service.

Page 2

To give you some brief background which gives rise to the
forgoing request for opinion, I am currently the owner and managing
broker of what I like to believe is an active local real estate
firm in the Yuba City area with offices at 1435 Butte House Road
which business is a member of the local multiple listing service. I
have owned and managed that business now for several years. I
currently serve on the Yuba City Planning Commission as a member of
that commission and have now for several years.

Differences of opinion exist between a member of the Yuba City
Council and myself with reference to specifically what does
constitute a conflict of interest within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act of 1974. Shortly after the last election that a
Council member asked for my resignation. That council person
charged that was "there is no way that you can be a Planning
Commissioner while being a real estate broker without there being
a conflict of interest." That council persons position was
essentially that as a member of the Commission voting on zoning,
subdivision and other issues which necessarily impacted to some
extent growth in the community and as a member of the real estate
profession I somehow had some inherent conflict or some inherent
incompatibility.

That charge prompted me to seek counsel with the contract City
Attorney regarding this matter and he reviewed with me, at some
length, the various conflict of interest statutes and specifically
the provisions of Government Code Section 87100 et seq as to what
constitutes a conflict of interest and as to what specifically
constitutes a material financial interest in a decision that would
come before the Commission. As a result of such discussion there
was an instance where, based upon the City Attorney's advice, a
matter was reagendized with myself abstaining from any vote on the
issue because of a potential conflict of interest and the record
was so clarified and that potential conflict of interest placed on
the record.

I have been extremely sensitive to the conflict of interest
issue since that time and have in any case where I felt such a
conflict existed abstained and foregone any decision making and/or
participation in the decision making process.

Being particularly sensitive to the issue and recognizing that
my voting record on matters that come before the Planning
Commission are now being closely monitored, it is important for my
own piece of mind to have some clarification at least in a general
sense so that this issue does not somehow get out of hand and be
blown totally out of proportion.

Page 3

What I have specifically in mind is that as you can
appreciate, various types of projects come before the Commission
for approval from time to time. Some of those projects are
subdivision approval projects at the tentative map stage, general
plan amendments, rezoning projects, permit applications, plan
development approvals, variance applications and the like. I am
quick to recognize that there are obvious direct and/or indirect
economic interests which would preclude my participation in
individual projects. Clearly property in which I would have an
interest or in which I would have a leasehold interest or in which
I would have some financial interest in the business entity that
would be seeking to develop the property, the subject of the
application, or in which for example I have had business dealings
or ongoing business dealings with developers of a particular
project which would constitute a source of income to me within the
meaning of the conflict of interest statutes. Those instances
obviously I would not participate in nor would I attempt to
influence the decision making process. There are, however,
situations that I can foresee where I have voted on a particular
planning issue in which I had no economic interest, either direct
or indirect, at the time the decision making process was involved
nor in which I anticipated, suspected or foresaw any future
economic interest nor one in which I was promised or assured of any
future economic interest and then subsequently either myself or one
of my agents had an opportunity to either represent a buyer in the
multiple listings service, which is a cooperative service in
connection with a sale on a piece of property that was the subject
of that prior development, or for that matter to take a subsequent
listing of that property.

What concerns me now is that somehow somebody will argue that
my subsequent contact with that property or some portion of that
property that was the subject of a prior approval in which I
participated at the time I had no direct or indirect economic
interest is now going to be construed as some sort of a conflict
and will somehow be claimed to taint my prior participation in the
planning process on that project and that this would now somehow
preclude me from either listing that property for sale or resale or
some portion thereof or representing a buyer in the multiple
listings service in connection with that property or some portion
thereof.

I would like to have the issue clarified for me from the Fair
Political Practices Commission's standpoint and at the same time
would like to have the general question answered on which a prior
charge has been leveled that somehow real estate brokers and/or
owners and/or managers of real estate firms are somehow inherently
precluded from serving their community as a member of a Planning
Commission body on the theory that there is somehow some inherent
conflict of interest between the two (2) positions.

Page 4

Your early response to these questions would be appreciated so
that hopefully the air can be cleared in the community on this
issue.

Very truly yours,

Lee E. Welch

LEW/lev

sections.question:
1. Does my occupation as a real estate broker who owns and manages a local real estate firm in the Yuba City area and at the same time serving as a member of the Yuba City Planning Commission in and of itself and without regard to specific projects, applications, rezonings, permit approvals, subdivision approvals and the like, namely in a vacuum, constitute a conflict of interest which would effectively preclude me from serving on the Commission on a theory that there is somehow some inherent incompatibility or conflict between serving on a local planning commission and at the same time owning and being the managing broker of an active local real estate firm.

2. Assuming that after I have voted on a particular planning issue in which I was satisfied that I had no economic interest, either direct or indirect, at the time the decision was made in that particular planning, zoning or subdivision matter and parenthetically one in which I neither anticipated, suspected, was promised or assured of any future financial source of income and subsequently either one of my real estate agents and/or myself for that matter had occasion to represent a buyer in the multiple listings service cooperative sale on the project that was the subject of the prior approval and/or somehow subsequently became involved in a subsequent listing of that property or some portion thereof for sale or for resale, would that somehow taint my prior participation in that planning decision so as to now constitute a conflict of interest which would somehow preclude me or prohibit me from participating in the listing of that property or the sale of that property under the multiple listing service.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a real estate broker who owns and manages a local real estate firm serve on a planning commission without an inherent conflict of interest, and does subsequent real estate activity on properties previously voted on create a retroactive conflict of interest?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or short answer section present. Document dated October 21, 1991. Requestor seeks clarification on Political Reform Act provisions regarding conflicts of interest and the compatibility of serving as both a planning commissioner and real estate broker.

embedding.summary: Lee E. Welch, a real estate broker and Yuba City Planning Commission member, requests an FPPC advisory opinion on whether his dual roles constitute an inherent conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and whether subsequent real estate transactions involving properties he previously voted on would taint his prior participation.
